regions/southeast.json.languageNotes: The Southern Vietnamese dialect differs noticeably from Northern Vietnamese, with softer tones and different vocabulary that can be challenging for learners trained in the Northern standard. Useful phrases include 'Xin chào' (hello), 'Cảm ơn bạn' (thank you), and 'Bao nhiêu tiền?' (how much money?), though English is increasingly spoken in Ho Chi Minh City among younger people and in business districts.

places.name: Ho Chi Minh City
places.description: Vietnam's largest city and economic hub, featuring colonial architecture, bustling markets, and vibrant street life. Essential for experiencing modern Vietnam and visiting the War Remnants Museum and Reunification Palace.
imageQueries: Ho Chi Minh City skyline night

activities.detail: A sobering and comprehensive museum documenting the Vietnam War through photographs, weapons, and survivor testimonies that provides essential historical context.
The iconic former presidential residence where the war ended in 1975, featuring impressive 1960s architecture and guided tours through state rooms and underground tunnels.
A historic covered market in District 1 buzzing with vendors selling souvenirs, silk, spices, and street food in a quintessentially chaotic Vietnamese shopping experience.
The city's tallest building offers 360-degree views of Ho Chi Minh City's skyline and the Saigon River from the 49th floor.
An extensive network of underground tunnels used during the Vietnam War, now accessible as a historical site where visitors can crawl through sections and learn about Viet Cong tactics.
A richly decorated traditional Chinese-influenced pagoda with intricate carvings and statues, offering a peaceful escape and insight into local spiritual practices.
Evening boat tours along the Saigon River provide views of colonial architecture and modern development while experiencing local waterfront life.

food.detail: Vietnam's iconic beef or chicken noodle soup served with fresh herbs, lime, and chili, best enjoyed at street stalls for an authentic breakfast experience.
A Vietnamese sandwich combining crusty French baguette with pâté, cold cuts, pickled vegetables, and cilantro, reflecting the city's colonial culinary heritage.
Broken rice served with grilled pork chop, egg, and pickled vegetables, a beloved local comfort food that is affordable and deeply Vietnamese.
A Cambodian-influenced clear broth soup with pork, shrimp, and tapioca pearls that is a Ho Chi Minh City specialty, reflecting the city's diverse heritage.
Guided evening walking tours through neighborhoods like District 1 or Binh Thanh sampling grilled items, spring rolls, and local specialties from street vendors.

culture.detail: Haggling over prices is expected and even enjoyable at Ben Thanh Market and street stalls; start at 50% of the asking price and work upward.
Motorbikes dominate traffic; always look both directions when crossing streets and respect the flow rather than trying to rush, as drivers are accustomed to navigating crowds.
Remove shoes before entering temples or pagodas, dress modestly, and be quiet during prayers; photography may be restricted in certain sacred areas.
Many small vendors and markets prefer cash in Vietnamese Dong; carry small denominations as vendors often claim they lack change for large notes.

places.name: Mekong Delta
places.description: A vast network of rivers, canals, and islands producing Vietnam's rice and fruit, with a distinctly rural character and unique boat-based culture. Visit for floating markets, fruit orchards, and traditional village experiences.

activities.detail: Visit Vietnam's most iconic floating market at dawn where boats laden with fruit and vegetables trade directly from vessel to vessel.
Take a guided boat journey through narrow canals and waterways to observe daily river life, traditional fish farms, and village settlements.
Explore tropical fruit gardens growing dragon fruit, mango, and coconut, often with opportunities to pick fruit and taste fresh juices.
Wander through this bustling traditional market to see local produce, freshly caught seafood, and regional handicrafts.
Watch artisans craft traditional coconut candy and rice paper products, with tastings and opportunities to purchase souvenirs.
Paddle a small wooden sampan through narrow waterways lined with houses, gardens, and local fishing spots.
Visit this protected island in Gao Giong to observe native bird species and experience pristine delta wetlands.

food.detail: A local specialty sandwich filled with grilled catfish, herbs, and pickled vegetables on crispy French-style bread.
Broken rice served with grilled catfish, crispy egg, and fresh vegetables—a regional staple reflecting the delta's agricultural abundance.
Freshwater prawns caught locally and grilled or boiled with herbs, showcasing the delta's exceptional aquaculture.
A tangy freshwater crab and tomato soup that's light, aromatic, and commonly served in family meals throughout the delta.
Homemade jams and candied fruits made from local dragon fruit, mango, and papaya, often sold at markets and orchards.
A famous sweet pastry from Sa Đéc filled with mung bean, pork fat, and salted egg yolk, traditionally given as gifts.

culture.detail: Most locals navigate by boat rather than car, so embrace water travel and wear comfortable shoes suitable for boarding small vessels.
Floating markets operate from 5 to 7 a.m., so join an early organized tour or hire a private boat to witness authentic trading before crowds arrive.
Villages remain largely agrarian with limited modern amenities; be respectful when photographing locals, ask permission, and avoid excessive displays of wealth.
The Mekong floods seasonally (May-October), which affects water levels and activities, so plan accordingly or visit in the dry season for easier navigation.

places.name: Cần Thơ
places.description: The largest city in the Mekong Delta with excellent access to floating markets and island orchards via boat tours. A gateway for exploring delta life and experiencing authentic local culture.

activities.detail: Visit the largest and most vibrant floating market in the Mekong Delta at dawn, where vendors sell produce and goods directly from boats.
Explore a quieter, more authentic floating market experience with fewer tourists and traditional wooden boats selling fruits and vegetables.
Stroll along the scenic Hậu River waterfront with evening views, local street food vendors, and relaxing atmosphere.
Take a boat tour through nearby island orchards to pick and taste exotic fruits like dragon fruit, mango, and coconut directly from the source.
Learn about the region's history, ecology, and traditional ways of life through exhibits on delta culture and environment.
Visit this rural island to experience authentic delta village life, traditional crafts, and local homestays with local families.
Take an evening boat tour along the Hậu River to watch the sunset while observing daily delta life and passing traditional wooden houses.

food.detail: A thick, chewy tapioca noodle soup with pork, shrimp, and local herbs that is the city's signature dish.
Broken rice served with grilled chicken, crispy shallots, and pickled vegetables, a beloved local breakfast staple.
Crispy Vietnamese crêpes filled with shrimp, pork, and bean sprouts, traditionally eaten in the Mekong Delta region.
Caramelized catfish cooked in clay pots with fish sauce and spices, showcasing the delta's abundant freshwater fish.
Fresh jackfruit served in sweetened coconut milk, a refreshing local dessert reflecting the region's tropical fruit abundance.

culture.detail: Visit floating markets before 7 AM to experience the busiest trading activity and authentic local commerce before tourist crowds arrive.
When taking boat tours, ask permission before photographing vendors, be mindful of boat speed in crowded market areas, and negotiate tour prices beforehand.
The Mekong Delta's flooding cycles and water management deeply influence daily life; respect local knowledge about seasonal changes and water levels when exploring.
Bring small Vietnamese dong bills for floating market vendors and local eateries, as many traditional merchants do not accept cards or large denominations.

places.name: Vũng Tàu
places.description: A beach resort town south of Ho Chi Minh City with relaxed coastal vibes, seafood restaurants, and mountain viewpoints including the iconic Christ the King statue. Popular for weekend escapes and water activities.

activities.detail: Visit the iconic 32-meter white statue atop Nho Mountain offering panoramic views of the coastline and city.
Relax and swim at the main urban beach with calm waters, seafood vendors, and evening promenades.
Stroll the scenic beachfront boulevard lined with restaurants, hotels, and views of the South China Sea.
Paddle through pristine mangrove ecosystems in a nearby preserve to spot birds and experience untouched nature.
Try jet skiing, parasailing, and banana boat rides at the main beach resort area.
Tour a local silk production facility to learn traditional weaving techniques and purchase handmade silk products.
Take a speedboat to this offshore island for snorkeling, swimming, and fresh seafood lunch.

food.text: Grilled Squid with Salt and Lime
food.detail: Fresh local squid charred on hot coals and seasoned simply with sea salt and fresh lime juice.
A tangy, rich soup made with locally caught blue crabs and sour tamarind, often served in a clay pot.
Whole fresh white fish steamed gently with ginger, scallions, and a light fish sauce dressing.
Cook-at-table experience with fresh shrimp, fish cakes, and vegetables in a fragrant broth.
Jumbo prawns encased in sea salt and roasted until the shells turn pink, served with dipping sauce.

culture.detail: Swim during daylight hours when lifeguards are present; avoid swimming after sunset due to strong currents.
Lightweight, breathable clothing is essential year-round; bring a hat and sunscreen as the sun is intense.
Eat seafood at restaurants near the harbor early in the day when catches are freshest and prices are fairest.
Remove shoes and dress modestly when entering local temples or shrines, and avoid touching Buddha statues.

places.name: Côn Đảo Islands
places.description: A remote archipelago with pristine beaches, clear waters, and historical prison ruins from the French colonial and war periods. Ideal for island hopping, snorkeling, and understanding Vietnam's turbulent history.

activities.detail: Tour the restored French colonial and Vietnamese War-era prison complex where thousands of political prisoners were held, now a sobering historical museum with preserved cells and exhibits.
Swim and snorkel in crystal-clear turquoise waters teeming with coral reefs and tropical fish just off this pristine white-sand beach on Côn Đảo's eastern shore.
Take a day trip via local fishing boat to nearby Ba Canh, Tây TDragons, and smaller islets to experience untouched beaches and isolated fishing villages.
Hike through dense mangrove forests and coastal trails in the protected national park to spot rare seabirds, monitor lizards, and panoramic island vistas.
Visit the southern tip of Côn Đảo island at sunset where two seas meet, offering dramatic views of the Mekong Delta meeting the ocean.
Walk through the stilted houses of this traditional fishing community to observe local life, interact with fishermen, and buy fresh catch for evening meals.

food.detail: Fresh local sea fish grilled whole with sea salt and lemongrass, a signature coastal dish reflecting the islands' fishing heritage.

food.text: Cơm gà An Nam (Hainanese chicken rice)
food.detail: Poached chicken served over fragrant rice cooked in broth, a Chinese-influenced dish particularly popular in the island's dining culture.
Sweet local mud crab steamed simply with ginger and scallions, showcasing the archipelago's abundant crustacean catches.
Whole lobsters brushed with butter and garlic, grilled over charcoal, available fresh at waterfront seafood restaurants.

food.text: Bánh canh cua (Crab tapioca cake soup)
food.detail: Thick soup made with tapioca pearls and fresh crab meat, a warming dish particularly enjoyed during cooler monsoon months.

culture.detail: When touring Phú Hải Prison, maintain silence and solemnity as many Vietnamese visitors come to honor relatives who died there; photographs should be taken discreetly.
Always wear a life jacket on island-hopping boats and confirm departure times the evening before, as weather and tide schedules dictate actual departure times throughout the day.
Local fishermen are friendly but reserved; a simple 'xin chào' (hello) and genuine interest in their work opens doors to authentic village experiences and local recommendations.
Monsoonal winds from May to September can make water activities dangerous and limit boat schedules, so plan visits during October to April for optimal conditions.

places.name: Mỹ Tho
places.description: A charming riverside town in the Mekong Delta known for coconut candy, small islands, and local boat tours. A good base for exploring the delta with a more intimate, less touristy atmosphere than Cần Thơ.

activities.detail: Take a guided boat tour to nearby islands like Thới Sơn or Phong Điền to see coconut processing, tropical orchards, and local fish farms.
Watch artisans make Mỹ Tho's famous coconut candy by hand, a sweet specialty that makes an ideal souvenir.
Explore this ornate 19th-century Buddhist temple with French colonial architecture and peaceful gardens.
Wander through the bustling wet market early morning to see fresh produce, live fish, and local ingredients unique to the delta.
Take an evening boat ride along the Tiền River to watch the sunset and see local fishermen casting their nets.
Visit this working floating market across the river where vendors trade fruits and goods from boats at dawn.

food.detail: A local staple of fragrant broken rice served with grilled pork chop, egg, and pickled vegetables, eaten at any time of day.
Local banh mi featuring crusty baguette filled with pâté, Vietnamese cold cuts, fresh herbs, and pickled vegetables from delta markets.
Sweet chewy candy made from fresh coconut pulp, a signature product of Mỹ Tho that has been made here for generations.
Light, crispy fried banana slices, often dusted with sugar and sesame, sold as a popular street snack throughout town.

food.text: Cá Kho Tộ (Braised Fish in Clay Pot)
food.detail: Fresh Mekong fish braised slowly in caramel and fish sauce, creating a rich, savory depth characteristic of delta cooking.
Tangy tamarind-based soup with fresh river fish, pineapple, and local vegetables, reflecting the delta's abundance of produce.

culture.detail: Respect the boatman's navigation and avoid sudden movements; wear a hat or bring sunscreen as the sun reflects intensely off the water.
Life in Mỹ Tho moves slowly and revolves around the river; plan activities around dawn and early morning when markets and activity peak.
Expect to negotiate prices at the central market and with street vendors, especially if buying multiple items or returning regularly.
Remove shoes and dress modestly when visiting temples like Vinh Trang; avoid loud behavior in these sacred spaces.

places.name: Châu Đốc
places.description: A multicultural riverside town near the Cambodian border with floating fish farms, ethnic Cham and Khmer communities, and mountain temples. Excellent for boat trips and understanding the delta's diversity.

activities.detail: Visit the extensive fish and crocodile farms on the Bassac River where locals raise catfish and other species in floating cages, and learn about aquaculture practices unique to the delta.
Explore the Cham ethnic community's neighborhoods, including their traditional wooden houses and mosques, to understand their distinct Islamic culture within Vietnam.
Hike to the summit of Nui Sam (Sam Mountain) to visit the Tay An Pagoda and other temples offering panoramic views of the Mekong Delta and Cambodia.
Take a early morning boat journey along the Bassac River to witness local daily life, floating markets, and the landscape as fishermen and farmers begin their work.
Wander through the bustling waterfront market packed with fresh produce, fish, local crafts, and street food stalls serving the multicultural community.
Visit traditional Khmer Buddhist temples in and around Chau Doc to observe religious practices and architecture of the local Khmer minority.

food.detail: A specialty clear soup with pork, shrimp, and tapioca noodles originating from nearby Phnom Penh, reflecting Cambodian-Vietnamese culinary fusion.
Fresh catfish caught from local farms, grilled with herbs and served with dipping sauce, showcasing the river's abundant aquaculture.
Steamed or fried cakes made with flour and vegetables, a staple food in Muslim Cham communities for breakfast or snacks.
A pungent fermented shrimp paste served with fresh vegetables and herbs, essential to Mekong Delta dining and reflecting local fishing traditions.
Cooked in aromatic broths with lemongrass and chilies, commonly sold by street vendors near the river and market areas.

culture.detail: When visiting mosques and pagodas, remove shoes, dress modestly, and ask permission before photographing; these are active places of worship for Cham Muslim and Khmer Buddhist communities.
A simple 'Xin chào' (hello) or 'Cảm ơn' (thank you) in Vietnamese, or Islamic greetings like 'Assalamu alaikum' in Cham areas, shows respect and opens friendly interactions.
The river and canals are the lifeblood of Chau Doc; hiring a local guide with a boat is the best way to access communities and experience authentic delta life rather than roads alone.
Plan around Vietnamese Tet (lunar new year) for vibrant celebrations, or Ramadan for understanding Cham fasting traditions and festive iftar meals shared in the community.

places.name: Rạch Giá
places.description: A coastal gateway city in Kiên Giang Province serving as the departure point for Phú Quốc Island and Hạ Tiên. Important for its fresh seafood, local markets, and transport connections.

activities.detail: The bustling central market offers fresh seafood, tropical fruits, and local crafts where you can experience daily life and haggle for souvenirs.
Located in the city center, this museum exhibits artifacts related to local history, maritime trade, and the region's cultural heritage.
The main departure point for ferries to Phú Quốc Island, where you can watch boats and plan island excursions while enjoying waterfront views.
This iconic yellow bridge spanning the Cái Rồng River offers photo opportunities and connects the city center with local neighborhoods.
Stroll along the riverside promenade in the late afternoon to watch fishing boats return and enjoy the cooling breeze before dinner.
Visit nearby Buddhist pagodas such as Vĩnh Hưng Pagoda to observe religious practices and enjoy peaceful, ornately decorated temple grounds.

food.text: Bánh Hỏi with Shrimp and Herbs
food.detail: Ultra-thin rice noodle rolls served with fresh local shrimp, fresh herbs, and dipping sauce—a signature Kiên Giang specialty found at street stalls.
Fresh squid caught in local waters grilled over charcoal and served with lime and chili, best enjoyed at waterfront seafood restaurants.
A savory broth combining freshwater crabs and pork belly with local herbs, commonly served for breakfast at local noodle shops.
Rice porridge simmered with freshwater snails and ginger, offering a warming, protein-rich meal popular in early morning at street vendors.
Rạch Giá is famous for producing high-quality fish sauce (nước mắm); visitors can tour small family factories to see the fermentation process.

culture.detail: Bargaining is expected and appreciated in local markets—politely counter offers by 20–30% and smile to negotiate better prices.
The city's rhythm follows fishing seasons and tides; early mornings and late afternoons are liveliest when boats depart and return.
When visiting pagodas, wear long pants or skirts and cover shoulders as a sign of respect for religious spaces.
Arrive early for ferry departures to Phú Quốc, keep luggage secure, and expect crowded conditions during peak travel times.

places.name: Hạ Tiên
places.description: A quiet border town near Cambodia with limestone cliffs, fishing villages, and scenic boat routes to nearby islands and caves. Perfect for tranquility and exploring the wild western edge of the delta.

activities.detail: A stunning cave temple built into a limestone cliff overlooking the Giang Thanh River, accessible by boat from the town center.
A quiet crescent beach just outside town with soft sand and views of fishing boats, ideal for swimming and sunset walks.
Take a ferry or speedboat to nearby Phu Quoc Island to explore pristine beaches, pepper plantations, and pearl farms.
Join local fishermen on traditional wooden boats to experience daily life, see floating fish farms, and explore mangrove channels.
A dramatic limestone monolith rising from the sea visible from the shore, best appreciated during early morning or sunset light.
Located in town, this small museum showcases local history, traditional fishing gear, and artifacts from the border region.
Paddle through narrow waterways connecting rice paddies, fruit gardens, and stilted houses in the surrounding countryside.

food.detail: Hạ Tiên's signature dish of clear broth with pork, shrimp, and thin noodles, often topped with pork liver and served with fresh herbs.
Steamed or grilled freshwater crabs caught daily from the Giang Thanh River, best eaten with salt, pepper, and lime.

food.text: Banh Chay Cua (crab and vegetable cake)
food.detail: Crispy pan-fried cakes filled with fresh crabmeat, wood ear mushrooms, and taro, served with sweet and sour dipping sauce.
Whole fish grilled over charcoal with turmeric, salt, and lemongrass, served with rice paper and fresh vegetables.
Jumbo freshwater shrimp from local ponds, grilled with garlic and chili or boiled simply to highlight their natural sweetness.

food.text: Sticky rice cakes with mango (Banh Chung Xoai)
food.detail: Sweet sticky rice wrapped in banana leaves with fresh mango pulp, a local dessert unique to the border region's fruit abundance.

culture.detail: Hạ Tiên sits just kilometers from Cambodia; avoid photographing military or border installations and do not cross unmarked boundaries.
Local fish and produce markets bustle before 8 AM with vendors and fishermen; this is the best time to experience authentic town life and negotiate prices.
When boarding fishing boats or ferries, remove shoes, move carefully, and ask permission before photographing fishermen or their catch.
When visiting Chua Hang or other religious sites, cover shoulders and knees out of respect, and ask before entering active worship areas.

places.name: Long Xuyên
places.description: An inland delta city serving as a regional hub with lively markets and local food culture, offering convenient access to nearby floating villages and orchards. Less touristy but authentic delta experience.

activities.detail: Join an early morning boat tour to observe the dynamic floating market where local vendors sell fish, produce, and goods directly from boats along the Hau River.
Visit traditional stilt house communities and fish farms in nearby An Phu district where residents live and work entirely on water.

activities.text: Long Xuyen Central Market (Cho Long Xuyen)
activities.detail: Explore the bustling indoor market selling fresh produce, meats, textiles, and local crafts that reflects authentic delta commerce and daily life.
Tour family-run orchards in the surrounding areas to pick seasonal fruits like mangoes, longans, and custard apples directly from trees.
Take a short journey to nearby Sam Mountain for panoramic views of the delta landscape and visit historic pagodas dotting the slopes.
Venture to the larger neighboring floating market for a more colorful display of wholesale fruit trading and riverside commerce.

food.detail: A signature clear broth noodle soup traditionally made with pork, shrimp, and squid, often eaten for breakfast as the city's most iconic dish.
Fragrant broken rice served with grilled pork, fried shallots, and a fried egg, commonly sold at street stalls as an affordable local staple.
Fresh baguettes filled with local pâté, Vietnamese cold cuts, pickled vegetables, and cilantro reflecting the region's French colonial influence.

food.text: Fresh river fish grilled in banana leaf
food.detail: Locally caught catfish or snakehead wrapped and grilled over charcoal, served with dipping sauce at riverside restaurants.

food.text: Fruit smoothies and sugar cane juice
food.detail: Freshly pressed drinks from local orchards and markets, particularly mango, longan, and papaya smoothies sold at busy food stalls.

culture.text: Market visits are early morning affairs
culture.detail: Arrive before 7 AM to experience the most active trading at Long Xuyen Central Market and floating markets when locals do their shopping.

culture.text: Respect for river life and traditions
culture.detail: The Mekong Delta's identity is tied to water; dress modestly when visiting villages and always ask permission before photographing residents or their homes.
While some establishments accept cards, small markets, street food vendors, and village visits require Vietnamese dong in cash for transactions.
Mango season peaks April-June and longan season July-September; plan orchard visits accordingly and expect long, hot hours of hand-harvesting work.

restaurants.name: Ngoc Suong

restaurants.name: Xu Restaurant

restaurants.name: Com Tam O Chi

regions.name: Northwest
regions.bestTimeToVisit: September to November offers cool weather and clear skies for trekking and exploring mountain villages.

regions.name: Northeast
regions.bestTimeToVisit: October to April provides pleasant weather for visiting Ha Long Bay and exploring limestone karsts.

regions.name: Red River Delta
regions.bestTimeToVisit: October to April is ideal with cooler temperatures and lower humidity for exploring the capital and delta.

regions.name: North Central Coast
regions.bestTimeToVisit: May to September offers warm weather and calm seas for beach activities and water sports.

regions.name: Central Highlands
regions.bestTimeToVisit: November to April provides cool, dry weather perfect for coffee plantations and outdoor activities.

regions.name: South Central Coast
regions.bestTimeToVisit: January to September features sunny skies and calm seas ideal for beach holidays and diving.

regions.name: Mekong Delta
regions.bestTimeToVisit: November to January offers dry weather and lower water levels for exploring floating markets and waterways.

regions.name: Southeast
regions.bestTimeToVisit: November to April offers cooler, drier weather for exploring the bustling metropolis and nearby attractions.

top10.name: Hanoi
top10.description: Vietnam's ancient capital on the Red River, blending centuries of history with vibrant street culture, French colonial architecture, and bustling markets. It's the natural entry point to Northwest Vietnam and offers temples, museums, water puppetry, and exceptional street food.

top10.name: Ho Chi Minh City
top10.description: Vietnam's largest city and economic hub, featuring colonial architecture, bustling markets, and vibrant street life. Essential for experiencing modern Vietnam and visiting the War Remnants Museum and Reunification Palace.

top10.name: Ha Long Bay
top10.description: A UNESCO World Heritage Site featuring thousands of limestone karsts rising from emerald waters, Ha Long Bay is Vietnam's most iconic natural wonder. Visitors can cruise among the dramatic islands, explore hidden caves, and swim in secluded lagoons.

top10.name: Hoi An
top10.description: A charming ancient town with well-preserved buildings featuring Vietnamese, Chinese, and French architectural influences, and famous for its lantern-lit streets and tailor shops. It attracts visitors seeking a slower pace and cultural immersion away from Vietnam's larger cities.

top10.name: Da Nang
top10.description: A modern coastal city serving as Vietnam's third-largest port and a hub for central Vietnam tourism, with access to nearby beaches and mountains. It has grown rapidly as a destination for business travelers and beachgoers visiting nearby attractions.

top10.name: Phu Quoc Island
top10.description: A picturesque island in the Gulf of Thailand known for pristine beaches, seafood restaurants, and lush hills, famous for its fish sauce production. It combines tropical relaxation with local culture and outdoor activities like snorkeling and hiking.

top10.name: Sapa
top10.description: A stunning mountain town in the Hoang Lien Son range at 1,600m elevation, famous for its terraced rice paddies, ethnic minority villages, and cool climate. It's the gateway to trekking through H'mong and other tribal settlements, offering some of Vietnam's most iconic landscapes and cultural experiences.

top10.name: Hue
top10.description: Vietnam's ancient imperial capital, home to the Citadel and royal tombs of the Nguyen Dynasty, showcasing feudal Vietnamese architecture and history. History buffs and cultural tourists visit to explore the nation's royal heritage and understand Vietnamese imperial traditions.

top10.name: Mekong Delta
top10.description: A vast network of rivers, canals, and islands producing Vietnam's rice and fruit, with a distinctly rural character and unique boat-based culture. Visit for floating markets, fruit orchards, and traditional village experiences.

top10.name: Nha Trang
top10.description: Vietnam's premier beach resort city with a long crescent bay, vibrant seafood restaurants, and water sports infrastructure. It's the main hub of the South Central Coast offering diving, island tours, and a lively nightlife scene.

guide/festivals.json.title: Festivals & events
guide/festivals.json.subtitle: 40 celebrations through the year — pick a day to see what's on.

festivals.name: Lunar New Year (Tet)
festivals.description: Vietnam's most important festival marking the start of the lunar calendar with family gatherings, traditional foods, and vibrant street celebrations across the country. It's a time of renewal and the highlight of the Vietnamese calendar.

festivals.name: Tet Nguyen Dan
festivals.description: The lunar new year festival with temple visits, flower markets, and traditional performances celebrating ancestor veneration and family unity.

festivals.name: Hung Kings' Festival
festivals.description: A sacred national festival honoring the legendary founders of Vietnam with temple ceremonies, processions, and traditional rituals at Hung Temple. It celebrates Vietnamese cultural identity and historical origins.

festivals.name: International Women's Day
festivals.description: A public holiday celebrating women with special events, flower gifts, and family gatherings throughout Vietnam.

festivals.name: Hoa Binh City Spring Festival
festivals.description: A regional spring celebration featuring local crafts, ethnic minority culture, and traditional performances in the mountainous northwest region.

festivals.name: Reunification Day
festivals.description: A national holiday commemorating the reunification of North and South Vietnam in 1975 with patriotic ceremonies and public celebrations.

festivals.name: Trang An Tourism Festival
festivals.description: A festival celebrating the UNESCO World Heritage site of Trang An with boat races, cultural performances, and eco-tourism activities.

festivals.name: Ghe Ngo Festival
festivals.description: A unique rowing festival featuring traditional dragon boat races and cultural performances celebrating the maritime heritage of Bac Ninh province.

festivals.name: Ha Long Bay Tourism Festival
festivals.description: A major festival celebrating Ha Long Bay UNESCO World Heritage site with water sports, seafood celebrations, and cultural events.

festivals.name: Labour Day
festivals.description: A public holiday honoring workers with parades and celebrations throughout Vietnam.

festivals.name: Vesak Festival
festivals.description: A major Buddhist celebration honoring Buddha's birthday with temple ceremonies, processions, and lantern displays across Vietnam.

festivals.name: Bat Trang Ceramic Festival
festivals.description: An ancient pottery village festival showcasing traditional ceramics with demonstrations, competitions, and artisan displays.

festivals.name: Hue Festival
festivals.description: A major biennial cultural festival in the ancient imperial city featuring traditional arts, water processions, and royal court performances. It's one of Vietnam's most prestigious cultural events.

festivals.name: Tam Coc Boat Festival
festivals.description: A scenic boat racing festival in the limestone karst region with traditional rowing competitions and cultural performances.

festivals.name: Nui Thanh Festival
festivals.description: A regional festival celebrating local traditions, ethnic minority crafts, and mountain culture in the central highlands.

festivals.name: Ban Flower Festival
festivals.description: An annual celebration of the Ban flower in the coffee-growing region with cultural performances and agricultural exhibitions.

festivals.name: Mid-Autumn Festival (Tet Trung Thu)
festivals.description: A beloved children's festival with lantern processions, mooncakes, and traditional performances celebrating the autumn moon across Vietnam.

festivals.name: National Day
festivals.description: Vietnam's independence day with grand parades, flag-raising ceremonies, and patriotic celebrations commemorating the 1945 revolution.

festivals.name: Dalat Flower Festival
festivals.description: Vietnam's premier flower festival in the cool highlands featuring stunning flower displays, horticultural competitions, and cultural shows. It attracts visitors from across the country.

festivals.name: Lim Festival
festivals.description: An ancient folk festival featuring traditional quan họ folk singing competitions where young singers in boats perform traditional duets.

festivals.name: Dong Da Hill Festival
festivals.description: A historical festival commemorating the 1789 Tet Offensive victory with cultural performances and historical reenactments.

festivals.name: Perfume Pagoda Festival
festivals.description: A major pilgrimage festival at a sacred mountain complex with religious ceremonies, boat processions, and traditional rituals honoring Buddhist deities.

festivals.name: Yen Tu Festival
festivals.description: A significant Buddhist pilgrimage festival at a sacred mountain featuring temple visits, meditation, and ancient religious traditions.

festivals.name: Bac Ha Flower Festival
festivals.description: A colorful festival celebrating the blooming of buckwheat flowers with ethnic minority culture, traditional crafts, and agricultural displays.

festivals.name: Sapa Love Market Festival
festivals.description: A unique cultural festival where H'mong and Dao ethnic minorities gather for traditional courtship rituals, music, and cultural exchanges.

festivals.name: Da Nang International Fireworks Festival
festivals.description: A spectacular pyrotechnic competition featuring international fireworks displays over the Han River with cultural performances and entertainment.

festivals.name: Hoi An Lantern Festival
festivals.description: A magical monthly celebration where thousands of silk lanterns light up the ancient town's streets and river creating an enchanting atmosphere.

festivals.name: Hoi An Traditional Crafts Festival
festivals.description: A celebration of traditional artisan crafts including silk weaving, lantern making, and woodcarving in the UNESCO World Heritage town.

festivals.name: Con Dao Festival
festivals.description: A historical festival commemorating Con Dao island's significance in Vietnam's independence movement with ceremonies and cultural events.

festivals.name: Mekong Delta Boat Festival
festivals.description: A vibrant festival celebrating the unique boat culture with traditional boat races, floating markets, and regional food celebrations.

festivals.name: Cai Rang Floating Market Festival
festivals.description: A unique celebration of the iconic floating market with early morning boat racing, traditional selling demonstrations, and local cuisine.

festivals.name: Chau Doc Fish Festival
festivals.description: A festival celebrating the Mekong Delta's fishing heritage with boat races, fish cooking competitions, and cultural performances.

festivals.name: Nha Trang Sea Festival
festivals.description: A coastal celebration featuring water sports, seafood festivals, and cultural performances honoring the city's maritime heritage.

festivals.name: Quy Nhon Fishing Festival
festivals.description: A celebration of the fishing community with boat blessings, seafood markets, and traditional maritime ceremonies.

festivals.name: Phu Quoc Night Market Festival
festivals.description: A food and cultural festival showcasing the island's seafood, local crafts, and vibrant night market atmosphere.

festivals.name: Ho Chi Minh City Festival
festivals.description: A major urban festival featuring street performances, fireworks, water processions, and cultural celebrations across the city.

festivals.name: Chu A Duong Wine Festival
festivals.description: A unique festival celebrating rice wine and ethnic minority traditions with tastings, cultural performances, and traditional ceremonies.

festivals.name: Kon Ha Nah Festival
festivals.description: An ethnic Jarai festival celebrating spring planting season with traditional rituals, music, and agricultural ceremonies.

festivals.name: Teachers' Day
festivals.description: A public holiday honoring teachers with school celebrations and ceremonies throughout Vietnam.

festivals.name: Christmas and New Year Celebrations
festivals.description: Though not a traditional Vietnamese holiday, Christmas and New Year are celebrated with vibrant street decorations, shopping festivals, and cultural performances especially in Ho Chi Minh City.

guide/history.json.title: A Short History of Vietnam
guide/history.json.subtitle: From its beginnings to today — 13 eras that shaped it.

items.label: Hunter-Gatherers of Southeast Asia
items.summary: Early humans inhabit the Red River Delta, hunting, fishing, and gathering in the tropical forests.
items.text: The earliest evidence of human habitation in Vietnam dates back to the Paleolithic period, with stone tools and cave paintings scattered across the landscape. These hunter-gatherers adapted brilliantly to the monsoon climate and river systems that would define Vietnamese civilization for millennia. They developed sophisticated knowledge of seasonal fish runs and forest resources, establishing the foundation for later agricultural societies. The Red River Delta, in what is now northern Vietnam, became increasingly important as populations grew and communities began experimenting with early forms of cultivation.

items.label: Bronze Age Cultures
items.summary: Bronze-working societies emerge, establishing hierarchical kingdoms and long-distance trade networks.
items.text: Around 2000 BC, bronze technology arrived in Vietnam, transforming tools, weapons, and religious artifacts. The Đông Sơn culture, flourishing in the Red River Delta from roughly 1000 to 200 BC, created elaborate bronze drums decorated with intricate geometric patterns that remain among Vietnam's most prized archaeological treasures. These drums were not mere musical instruments but symbols of power and status, suggesting sophisticated social organization and ritual practices. Trade routes connected Vietnamese bronze workers to China and Southeast Asia, and Chinese influences began creeping southward during this period.

items.label: Chinese Domination
items.summary: China conquers Vietnam and rules for over a thousand years, imposing language, bureaucracy, and culture.
items.text: In 111 BC, Han Dynasty forces conquered Vietnam and incorporated it as the province of Giao Chỉ, beginning a millennium of Chinese rule. The Chinese brought bureaucratic systems, Confucian philosophy, wet-rice agriculture, and the Vietnamese language written in Chinese characters. Despite repeated Chinese campaigns to suppress Vietnamese culture and identity, the Vietnamese people gradually synthesized Chinese and indigenous traditions into something distinctly their own. Periodic rebellions flared throughout this era, most famously the Trưng Sisters' uprising in 40 AD, showing that subjugation sparked constant resistance.

items.label: Independence and the Đinh Dynasty
items.summary: Vietnam breaks free from China and establishes its first independent native dynasties.
items.text: In 938 AD, Đinh Bộ Lĩnh defeated a weakened Chinese force at the Battle of Bạch Đằng River, securing Vietnamese independence after over a thousand years of Chinese rule. He established the Đinh Dynasty, but it collapsed quickly after his death, replaced by the Lê Dynasty, which solidified state institutions and Confucian governance structures. The early independent period saw rapid territorial expansion southward, as Vietnamese rulers extended their control into former Champa lands. Buddhism and Confucianism both flourished, with monasteries and temples becoming centers of learning and influence.

items.label: The Lý Dynasty Golden Age
items.summary: The Lý Dynasty creates a stable, prosperous Vietnam famous for art, literature, and military prowess.
items.text: The Lý Dynasty, which ruled for over two centuries, is remembered as Vietnam's golden age of classical civilization. Emperors like Lý Thánh Tông established Confucian-based civil service exams, patronized Buddhist monasteries, and oversaw remarkable achievements in poetry, calligraphy, and architecture. The dynasty successfully repelled Chinese invasions in 1075 and 1287, proving that Vietnam could defend its independence militarily. The first Vietnamese university, the Temple of Literature in Hanoi, was founded in 1070, becoming a symbol of Vietnamese intellectual aspiration and excellence.

items.label: The Trần Dynasty and Tran Hung Dao
items.summary: The Trần Dynasty heroically defeats three Mongol invasions through tactical genius and national unity.
items.text: After the Lý Dynasty's decline, the Trần Dynasty emerged and faced its greatest test when Kublai Khan's Mongol armies invaded Vietnam three times in the 13th century. General Trần Hưng Đạo, celebrated as one of history's greatest military minds, repulsed the Mongols through brilliant tactics including naval ambushes that destroyed enemy fleets. The famous 1287 Battle of Bạch Đằng River saw Trần forces impale Mongol ships on hidden stakes, becoming a legendary moment in Vietnamese national identity. The Trần Dynasty also continued southward expansion, gradually absorbing territories of the declining Champa Kingdom in a centuries-long process called Nam Vị.

items.label: The Lê Dynasty Restoration
items.summary: The Lê Dynasty restores Vietnamese rule after Chinese Ming occupation and completes southern expansion.
items.text: When Chinese Ming forces occupied Vietnam from 1407 to 1427, Lê Lợi led a brilliant guerrilla resistance that finally expelled them, restoring Vietnamese independence under the Lê Dynasty. The Lê period, particularly under Lê Thánh Tông, saw massive territorial expansion southward as the Vietnamese absorbed the Champa Kingdom and pushed toward the Mekong Delta. Vietnamese culture flourished with literature, history-writing, and arts reaching new heights, though the dynasty gradually weakened over centuries. By the 1600s, power had devolved to regional lords, though the Lê emperors remained as ceremonial figures, presaging civil war.

items.label: The Trinh-Nguyen Civil War
items.summary: Two powerful families tear Vietnam apart in a century-long conflict that reshapes the country's geography.
items.text: The breakdown of Lê authority led to the Trịnh-Nguyễn War, in which two regional families contested control of Vietnam from 1627 to 1677, then again from 1627 onwards in various forms. The Trịnh family controlled the north while the Nguyễn family, initially weaker, retreated southward and used the chaos to consolidate control over the Mekong Delta and Cambodia. This conflict, lasting roughly 150 years with interruptions, devastated the countryside but ultimately resulted in the Nguyễn gaining the southern third of modern Vietnam. When the conflict finally ended, Vietnam was geographically much larger but politically fractured, setting the stage for further upheaval.

items.label: The Tây Sơn Rebellion and Reunification
items.summary: A peasant-based rebellion unites Vietnam briefly before the Nguyễn Dynasty establishes lasting rule.
items.text: The Tây Sơn Rebellion, a brilliant military campaign by three brothers from a village in south-central Vietnam, swept away both the Trịnh and Nguyễn families by 1777 and reunified the country. The Tây Sơn, whose leader Quang Trung defeated a Chinese invasion in 1789 in a stunning display of tactical skill, redistributed land to peasants and promised social reform. However, the rebellion was ultimately fragile, and the last surviving Nguyễn prince, Gia Long, with French assistance, defeated the Tây Sơn by 1802 and established the Nguyễn Dynasty. Gia Long crowned himself emperor and moved the capital to Huế in central Vietnam, establishing a unified state that would face entirely new challenges from European colonialism.

items.label: French Colonization
items.summary: France conquers Vietnam and rules for nearly a century, extracting resources and suppressing Vietnamese identity.
items.text: Starting in 1858, French military forces, often aided by Catholic missionaries and local collaborators, conquered Vietnam in stages over two decades. By 1887, all of Vietnam was part of the French Indochina colony, along with Cambodia and Laos, administered as a French possession designed primarily to extract wealth. The French imposed their language, bureaucracy, and educational systems while suppressing Vietnamese traditions and siphoning resources for metropolitan France. Vietnamese nationalists organized resistance movements throughout the colonial period, from early armed uprisings to later communist organizations that would eventually triumph.

items.label: August Revolution and First Indochina War
items.summary: Ho Chi Minh declares independence; France fights a losing war before Vietnam achieves freedom.
items.text: After Japanese occupation during World War II, Ho Chi Minh's Việt Minh forces declared Vietnamese independence on September 2, 1945, establishing the Democratic Republic of Vietnam in Hanoi. France refused to accept independence and sent troops to reassert colonial control, sparking the First Indochina War, a brutal conflict lasting until 1954. The Việt Minh, using guerrilla tactics and popular support, gradually wore down French forces, culminating in the shocking French defeat at Điện Biên Phủ in May 1954. The Geneva Accords that followed temporarily divided Vietnam at the 17th parallel, promising reunification elections that never occurred.

items.label: The Vietnam War and American Intervention
items.summary: The Cold War splits Vietnam; North and South clash while the US supports the South in a devastating conflict.
items.text: After the Geneva Accords, South Vietnam, led by anti-communist Ngo Dinh Diem, refused to hold reunification elections, supported by the United States, which feared communist expansion. The North, under Ho Chi Minh, built a communist state while the South descended into political instability and corruption despite American military and economic aid. Tensions escalated into the Vietnam War, which saw American forces commit hundreds of thousands of troops and wage a brutal campaign of bombing and ground warfare against communist forces. The war killed millions of Vietnamese civilians and soldiers and devastated the landscape with chemical weapons, becoming one of the 20th century's most destructive conflicts.

items.label: Reunification and Communist Vietnam
items.summary: North Vietnam wins the war, unifying the country under communist rule but suffering decades of hardship before economic reform.
items.text: In April 1975, North Vietnamese forces captured Saigon, reunifying Vietnam under communist control and ending the war that had devastated the nation. The immediate aftermath brought terrible suffering, including re-education camps for those associated with the South and a massive refugee exodus of boat people fleeing persecution and poverty. Vietnam faced international isolation, economic collapse, and conflict with China and Cambodia throughout the 1970s and 1980s. Beginning in 1986, the government introduced Đổi Mới economic reforms that gradually opened Vietnam to market mechanisms while maintaining Communist Party political control, transforming Vietnam into one of Asia's fastest-growing economies today.

guide/food.json.title: Food & Drink
guide/food.json.subtitle: How to eat well in Vietnam — customs, dishes and where to go.

items.label: Cà Phê Đen Đá
items.text: Strong black coffee served over ice with a metal filter (phin) dripping slowly onto your table. This is the quintessential Vietnamese coffee experience—bitter, intense, and meant to be lingered over for 10-15 minutes while the ice melts.

items.label: Cà Phê Sữa Đá
items.text: Black coffee sweetened with sweetened condensed milk and served over ice. This is the most popular way Vietnamese people drink coffee, and it's what you'll find in nearly every café and street stall.

items.label: Egg Coffee in Hanoi
items.text: Cà phê trứng (egg coffee) is a Hanoi specialty made with dark coffee topped with a creamy mixture of egg yolk and condensed milk. Try it at Café Giang near Hoàn Kiếm Lake, the birthplace of this drink, though it's become touristy—locals still prefer simpler preparations.

items.label: Café Culture Timing
items.text: Vietnamese people sit in cafés for hours nursing a single coffee, treating them as social spaces rather than grab-and-go stops. Expect to stay 30 minutes minimum, and don't feel rushed by café staff.

items.label: Street Coffee vs. Café Chains
items.text: Avoid international chains and head to unmarked street-level coffee spots where locals gather. A proper Vietnamese coffee costs 15,000-30,000 VND ($0.70-$1.25), while tourist-oriented cafés charge 2-3 times more for the same thing.

items.label: Morning Phở Stalls
items.text: Phở is traditionally a breakfast food, so eat it between 6-10am at hole-in-the-wall stalls with plastic stools and a single pot simmering behind the counter. These are always cheaper and more authentic than restaurant versions.

items.label: Night Markets & Food Streets
items.text: Hoi An's Night Market, Hanoi's Tà Hiên Street, and Ho Chi Minh's Ben Thanh night food area come alive after 7pm with vendors selling grilled meats, bánh mì, and fresh spring rolls. Arrive hungry and expect to eat across 5-10 different stalls.

items.label: Hygiene & Safety
items.text: Busy stalls with high turnover are generally safe; avoid places with food sitting out all day or where you can't see the cooking. Stick to cooked foods if you have a sensitive stomach, and peel your own fruit when possible.

items.label: Family-Run Restaurants
items.text: Look for places with a plastic table outside a home, a handwritten menu, and where the owner's family is also eating—these serve authentic, affordable food at 30,000-60,000 VND ($1.25-$2.50) per person. Avoid anywhere with color-printed menus and tourist prices.

items.label: Cash is Essential
items.text: Small street stalls and local restaurants rarely accept cards; carry small bills (10,000-50,000 VND notes) as vendors often can't make change for large denominations. Only upscale restaurants in major cities accept cards reliably.

items.label: Eat When Locals Eat
items.text: Breakfast is 6-9am, lunch is 11:30am-1pm, and dinner is 6-8pm. Restaurants are often closed between 2-5pm, and many street stalls shut down by 9pm. Eating outside these windows means cold food and empty restaurants.

items.label: Phở
items.text: Vietnam's national soup of rice noodles in aromatic beef or chicken broth, topped with fresh herbs, lime, and chilli. Phở Bò (beef) is richer and more traditional, while Phở Gà (chicken) is lighter; order it at breakfast for the freshest stock.

items.label: Bánh Mì
items.text: A French-Vietnamese sandwich on crispy baguette with pâté, cold cuts, pickled daikon, cucumber, cilantro, and chilli mayo. A perfect bánh mì from a street vendor costs 15,000-25,000 VND and rivals any fancy lunch elsewhere.

items.label: Bún Chả
items.text: Grilled pork patties and pork belly served with rice vermicelli, fresh herbs, and dipping sauce (nước chấm). This Hanoi specialty tastes even better when you roll it all in fresh rice paper—it's street food elevated.

items.label: Cơm Tấm
items.text: Broken rice (tấm) topped with a fried egg, grilled pork chop (thịt nướng), and pickled vegetables, finished with sweet and savory sauce. It's humble and cheap (20,000-30,000 VND) but deeply satisfying, especially at lunch.

items.label: Gỏi Cuốn
items.text: Fresh spring rolls made with rice paper, herbs, shrimp, and vermicelli, served with peanut dipping sauce. These are light, refreshing, and appear as street-food snacks and restaurant starters alike.

items.label: Mực Nướng
items.text: Grilled squid seasoned simply with salt, pepper, and served with lime and chilli—a must-eat in coastal areas like Nha Trang and Da Nang, especially at beachfront stalls at sunset.

items.label: Chè
items.text: Vietnamese sweet dessert soup or pudding made from beans, coconut, tapioca, or fruit, served either hot or cold. It's a refreshing afternoon snack (10,000-15,000 VND) sold by street vendors with small carts.

items.label: No English? Use Your Phone
items.text: Point your phone camera at the menu and use Google Translate's camera feature, or ask the owner to write prices and dish names on paper. Most restaurant staff don't speak English outside tourist areas, but they're patient with gesturing.

items.label: Understanding Pricing
items.text: Local menus list prices per portion in Vietnamese Dong (VND). If you see a price without clear portion size, ask 'một suất?' (one serving?). Tourist restaurants sometimes charge by weight for grilled meats—confirm the price beforehand.

items.label: Meat Quality at Grilled Stalls
items.text: At bún chả or thịt nướng stalls, you can often point to the specific cut of meat you want grilled. Fatty pork (thịt ba chỉ) is the most flavorful; leaner cuts (thịt nạc) are cheaper but drier.

items.label: Vegetarian & Dietary Needs
items.text: Say 'ăn chay' (vegetarian) or 'không có thịt' (no meat), though understand that 'vegetarian' in Vietnam often includes fish sauce and shrimp paste. For strict vegetarian/vegan meals, visit Buddhist restaurants or mention 'chay hoàn toàn' (completely vegetarian).

items.label: Portion Sizes
items.text: Vietnamese portions are modest; one phở or main dish feeds one person, but rice dishes (cơm) and noodle soups are filling as single meals. Order multiple small dishes to share if dining with others—it's the traditional way.

items.label: Bia Việt
items.text: Vietnamese beer brands like Saigon, Hanoi, and 333 are light lagers brewed with rice, meant to be served ice-cold. A draft beer (bia hơi) costs 10,000-20,000 VND per glass and is incredibly popular at casual outdoor beer stalls (bia hơi).

items.label: Bia Hơi Culture
items.text: Bia hơi (fresh beer) stalls in Hanoi and Ho Chi Minh are social gathering spots where locals sit on tiny plastic stools, drink fresh draft beer, and eat simple snacks. It's cheap (15,000 VND), sociable, and the most authentic drinking experience.

items.label: Nước Mía
items.text: Fresh sugarcane juice pressed on the street in front of you, often mixed with lime or other fruit. It's incredibly refreshing (10,000-15,000 VND), hydrating, and sold by juice carts throughout the day.

items.label: Trà Đá
items.text: Iced tea, usually jasmine or lotus leaf, served free or very cheaply (5,000-10,000 VND) at meals. Always ask for it without sugar if you prefer unsweetened, as Vietnamese tea is often pre-sweetened.

items.label: Cơm Rượu
items.text: Rice wine consumed as a social drink at family meals or celebratory dinners, often shared from a communal glass. It's strong and meant to be sipped slowly with food—say 'một chút thôi' (just a little) if offered.

items.label: Avoiding Fake or Dangerous Alcohol
items.text: Stick to recognizable brand-name beer and spirits in bottles with intact seals. Homemade rice wine or unbranded spirits in tourist areas can be contaminated; avoid them unless you're in a trusted family setting.

items.label: Eating at the Table
items.text: Use chopsticks (đũa) for noodles and rice, and a spoon (muỗng) for soup—don't eat directly from a shared bowl. In family settings, elders serve themselves first and younger people wait to begin eating until they do.

items.label: The Spittoon & Fishbones
items.text: Vietnamese dining involves spitting bones and shells directly onto the table or into a provided bowl; it's not considered impolite. Don't be shocked when locals spit across the table or leave bones stacked on their plate.

items.label: Sharing Dishes & Rice
items.text: Meals are served family-style with multiple shared dishes in the center and rice on the side. Everyone takes a small portion of each dish onto their personal bowl of rice rather than eating from shared plates.

items.label: Toasting & Alcohol
items.text: When someone toasts 'Một, hai, ba, dô!' (one, two, three, cheers!), clink glasses and always drink. Refusing a toast can be seen as disrespectful in social or business meals, though saying 'tôi không uống rượu' (I don't drink alcohol) is acceptable.

items.label: Tipping & Bills
items.text: Tipping is not expected or traditional in Vietnam, though upscale restaurants (especially in tourist areas) may have a tip line on the bill. Small change is sometimes left as rounding, but it's not obligatory. The bill (tính tiền) should be asked for when you're ready to leave.

items.label: Eating with Your Hands
items.text: Sticky rice (xôi), spring rolls, and certain grilled meats are meant to be eaten by hand or rolled into rice paper. Don't hesitate to use your fingers—it's the intended way, and napkins (khăn ăn) should be available.

items.label: Health & Safety Gestures
items.text: It's polite to cover your mouth when eating or drinking, and to show interest by asking 'Ngon không?' (Is it delicious?). Complimenting the food is appreciated, though don't leave your chopsticks standing upright in rice—it resembles a funeral ritual.

items.label: Market Tours & Cooking Classes
items.text: Cities like Hanoi, Ho Chi Minh, and Hoi An offer half-day cooking classes (usually 200,000-400,000 VND) that include a morning market visit and hands-on instruction. These are tourist-oriented but genuinely educational for learning technique and ingredients.

items.label: Hoi An's Cooking Schools
items.text: Hoi An is Vietnam's cooking-class capital with dozens of schools offering small-group or private classes. Red Bridge Cooking School is well-known but pricey; smaller family-run schools are often better value and more intimate.

items.label: Street Food Tours
items.text: Guided street food tours in major cities take you to 5-10 local vendors in one evening, ending with a full meal for 400,000-600,000 VND. They're popular with tourists but do expose you to places you'd struggle to find alone.

items.label: Farm-to-Table Experiences
items.text: In rural areas like the Mekong Delta or northern provinces, stay at home-based guest houses where the owner's family cooks from their own garden or farm. This is the most authentic food experience and costs less than formal classes.

guide/transport.json.title: Getting Around
guide/transport.json.subtitle: Trains, roads, taxis and how to move around Vietnam.

items.label: Book through Vietnam Railways
items.text: Use the official Vietnam Railways website (dsvn.vn) or authorized agents like Baolau to book tickets in advance. Prices are fixed and cheaper than buying at stations, especially for popular Hanoi-Ho Chi Minh and Hanoi-Da Nang routes.

items.label: Overnight trains save time and money
items.text: The SE1/SE2 express between Hanoi and Ho Chi Minh City takes 30 hours but eliminates a hotel night. Soft sleeper cabins (4-bed) offer reasonable comfort; hard sleeper is crowded but budget-friendly.
Train schedules are frequently delayed by 2–6 hours; plan buffer time before connections. Stations lack English signage, so arrive early and ask staff to confirm your platform.

items.label: Bring food and water
items.text: Onboard dining is limited and overpriced; bring snacks and a water bottle. Toilets are basic but functional on most routes.

items.label: Hanoi & Ho Chi Minh metro systems are new
items.text: Hanoi's Line 2A (Cat Linh–Ha Dong) and Ho Chi Minh's Line 1 (Ben Thanh–Suoi Tien) are clean and efficient. Fares are fixed (20,000–30,000 VND) and much cheaper than taxis for longer distances.

items.label: Use Grab Bus for city routes
items.text: Grab Bus operates in Hanoi, Ho Chi Minh, and Da Nang with in-app booking and set pricing. Buses are air-conditioned and reliable, ideal for avoiding traffic stress.
Public buses are crowded during rush hours (7–9 AM, 5–7 PM) and drivers speak minimal English. Routes are not always marked clearly; ask locals or use Google Maps for directions.

items.label: Buy a transit card for convenience
items.text: Ho Chi Minh's Beecards and Hanoi's metro cards reduce queuing and offer a small discount. You can top them up at station kiosks.

items.label: Grab is safer than street taxis
items.text: The app is available nationwide and shows driver details, routes, and fixed prices upfront. Payments are cashless, eliminating scam risks and change disputes.
Unmarked taxis frequently overcharge tourists; some drivers disable meters deliberately. If using street taxis, confirm they activate the meter before departing or agree on a price beforehand.

items.label: Grab prices surge during peak hours
items.text: Book in the early morning or late afternoon for lower fares. Grab Pool (shared rides) is significantly cheaper if you don't mind waiting a few minutes.

items.label: Traffic accidents are common
items.text: Drivers often speed and ignore traffic signals; avoid riding during rain. Travel with your hotel's trusted taxi number rather than random street hails.

items.label: Reputable taxi companies include Mai Linh and Vinasun
items.text: Call or ask your hotel to book; these companies use meters and have complaint hotlines. You'll pay slightly more but avoid significant scams.

items.label: Driving is dangerous for tourists
items.text: Traffic rules are loosely enforced, motorcyclists ignore lanes, and accident rates are high. Unless you're an experienced driver in chaotic conditions, use Grab or hire a driver instead.

items.label: International Driving Permit required
items.text: You must carry an International Driving Permit alongside your home license. Vietnam does not recognize foreign licenses alone.
Police conduct frequent checkpoints and may demand bribes (50,000–100,000 VND); keep documents visible. Helmet use is mandatory for all riders; fines are 200,000–500,000 VND if not worn.

items.label: Hire a driver instead
items.text: Tour operators and apps like Grab arrange drivers for full-day or multi-day trips at reasonable rates (500,000–800,000 VND/day). This is safer and lets you relax.
Petrol stations are sparse outside cities; fill up in towns. Road signs lack English; GPS (Google Maps offline) is essential.

items.label: Main airports: Noi Bai, Tan Son Nhat, Da Nang
items.text: Noi Bai (Hanoi) and Tan Son Nhat (Ho Chi Minh) are the hubs for domestic and international flights. Da Nang airport is modern and handles flights to central Vietnam.

items.label: Book domestic flights online in advance
items.text: Low-cost carriers like VietJet Air and Bamboo Airways offer fares from 500,000–1,500,000 VND if booked weeks ahead. Last-minute bookings are expensive and flights often overbook.

items.label: Airport transfers via Grab or shuttles
items.text: Grab from airport to city center costs 150,000–250,000 VND depending on distance. Many hotels offer free or cheap shuttle services; ask when booking.
Arrive 2 hours early for domestic flights and 3 for international; lines are long during peak hours. Baggage allowances are strict; budget airlines charge 150,000–300,000 VND for excess.

items.label: Check visa requirements early
items.text: Many nationalities need advance visas; e-visas (25 USD) are processed online in 1–3 days. Don't rely on visa-on-arrival services at airports as queues are lengthy.

items.label: Mekong Delta and Ha Long Bay ferries are popular
items.text: Ha Long Bay offers tourist boat tours (500,000–2,000,000 VND/day) booked through agents or apps. Mekong Delta ferries are local transit but slow; longer distances are better by car.

items.label: River ferries in cities are cheap and scenic
items.text: Ho Chi Minh's Saigon River ferries cost 5,000–15,000 VND and connect Binh Thanh to District 1. They're slower than buses but offer a unique local experience.
Ferry schedules are irregular and often delayed; check locally before heading to the dock. Safety standards are basic; wear a life jacket if offered and avoid travel during storms.

items.label: Book Ha Long Bay tours with reputable operators
items.text: Avoid very cheap tours (under 800,000 VND) as they often involve overcrowding and poor facilities. Use established agents like Handspan Travel or Viettourist.

items.label: Buy train and bus tickets in advance online
items.text: Websites like 12Go.asia, Baolau, and Busbooking.com let you compare prices and book seats. Booking 2–4 weeks ahead guarantees availability and lower fares.

items.label: Tourist passes exist for some cities
items.text: Ho Chi Minh's Saigontourist Card offers discounts on attractions and transit. Hanoi doesn't have a city pass; pay per journey or use a transit card.

items.label: No-show policies are strict
items.text: Most train and bus bookings are non-refundable or charge 50% penalty if cancelled within 48 hours. Read terms carefully before confirming.

items.label: Keep all receipts and confirmation emails
items.text: Ticket agents sometimes ask for proof; digital confirmations are sufficient. Printed tickets are issued at stations or via email.
Avoid street ticket sellers; they often sell invalid or overpriced tickets. Always buy from official websites, authorized agents, or station counters.

items.label: Buy a local SIM card at airport or convenience stores
items.text: Viettel, Vinaphone, and Mobifone offer prepaid SIM cards (50,000–100,000 VND) with bundles of data and calls. Activation is instant; you need a passport copy.

items.label: Data-only plans are very cheap
items.text: A 30-day unlimited 4G plan costs 50,000–100,000 VND; voice calls are extra. This is ideal if you already have a home number via WhatsApp or Viber.

items.label: WiFi is widely available in cities
items.text: Hotels, cafes, restaurants, and shopping malls offer free WiFi; speeds are generally adequate. Password information is usually printed on receipts or available from staff.
Public WiFi in cafes is often slow and unsecured; avoid accessing banking or sensitive accounts. Use a VPN if handling private data.

items.label: Download maps and translation apps offline
items.text: Save Google Maps offline for your entire trip and use Google Translate's offline mode. This reduces data usage and helps navigate when signal is weak.
